articleTitle: ReqFusion: A Multi-Provider Framework for Automated PEGS Analysis Across Software Domains

content.technicalOverview: ReqFusion implements a multi-provider LLM ensemble architecture that leverages OpenAI GPT, Anthropic Claude, and Groq models for automated requirements extraction from heterogeneous document formats (PDF, DOCX, PPTX). The system employs the PEGS (Project, Environment, Goals, System) framework as a structured prompt engineering technique, transforming unstructured requirement extraction into four constrained sub-tasks with explicit semantic boundaries. A consensus mechanism aggregates outputs across providers using cross-model agreement scoring, serving as runtime verification to reduce hallucination rates. The architecture achieves domain-independent extraction through template-based prompt construction and implements bidirectional traceability linking between extracted requirements and downstream development artifacts.
content.problemContext: Traditional requirements engineering suffers from high labor costs, inconsistent extraction quality, and poor scalability across document types and domains. LLM-based approaches face significant challenges with hallucination, inconsistent output formatting, and poor performance on unstructured extraction tasks where semantic boundaries are unclear. Single-provider solutions introduce vendor lock-in and single points of failure, while generic prompting approaches lack the contextual cues necessary for accurate requirement categorization. Existing tools typically focus on narrow domain applications rather than providing comprehensive, cross-domain extraction capabilities with verifiable output quality.
content.coreInnovation: The key innovation lies in transforming requirement extraction from a single complex generation task into four semantically-bounded sub-tasks using PEGS framework constraints, reducing the LLM search space and providing implicit validation criteria. The multi-provider consensus mechanism implements runtime verification by leveraging model disagreement as a quality signal, with fallback mechanisms ensuring high availability. This approach achieves a 24% improvement in F1 score (0.88 vs 0.71) over generic prompting while providing 78% reduction in analysis time compared to manual methods, demonstrating that structured prompt engineering can significantly improve LLM reliability for enterprise requirements engineering workflows.
technicalMechanics: Document ingestion pipeline processes PDF, DOCX, and PPTX formats using format-specific parsers, extracting text while preserving structural metadata and section hierarchies for context-aware processing

PEGS-guided prompt construction generates four specialized prompts per document, each targeting specific requirement categories with explicit semantic constraints and validation criteria embedded in the prompt structure

Multi-provider orchestration layer implements parallel inference across OpenAI GPT, Anthropic Claude, and Groq models with request batching, rate limiting, and provider-specific error handling and retry logic
Consensus algorithm aggregates model outputs using weighted voting based on confidence scores, with disagreement thresholds triggering additional validation rounds or human review escalation
Requirement classification pipeline processes extracted requirements through functional/non-functional binary classification with confidence scoring and uncertainty quantification
Traceability matrix construction links extracted requirements to source document locations, timestamps, and model provenance for audit trails and change impact analysis
Hallucination detection employs cross-model consistency checking, semantic similarity scoring between provider outputs, and fact-checking against source document content
Output standardization ensures consistent requirement formatting across providers using template-based post-processing and schema validation with configurable output formats

productionConsiderations.scalability: The multi-provider architecture scales horizontally through provider load balancing but faces bottlenecks at API rate limits (OpenAI: 10K RPM, Anthropic: 5K RPM). Document processing scales linearly with document size, but consensus computation has O(n²) complexity for requirement cross-validation. Batch processing capabilities support enterprise-scale document volumes with configurable parallelism.
productionConsiderations.latency: End-to-end processing latency ranges 30-120 seconds per document depending on provider response times and consensus complexity. Real-time processing is limited by LLM inference latency (2-8 seconds per API call). Asynchronous processing with job queuing supports batch workflows but interactive use cases require latency optimization.
productionConsiderations.resourceRequirements: Minimal local compute requirements as inference is API-based, but requires reliable internet connectivity and API quota management across multiple providers. Storage needs scale with document corpus size and traceability metadata. Memory requirements are dominated by document parsing (up to 500MB for large PDFs) rather than model inference.
productionConsiderations.implementationComplexity: Moderate complexity requiring LLM API integration, document parsing libraries (pypdf2, python-docx, python-pptx), consensus algorithm implementation, and robust error handling across provider failures. Infrastructure needs include job queuing (Redis/Celery), database for traceability (PostgreSQL), and monitoring for API quota management.

content.applicationsAndImpact: Enterprise software development organizations can integrate ReqFusion into CI/CD pipelines for automated requirement validation and impact analysis, reducing manual review cycles from weeks to hours. Government contracting and RFP processing workflows benefit from automated requirement extraction and compliance checking against predefined standards. Large-scale software modernization projects can leverage the traceability features for legacy system analysis and migration planning. The 78% time reduction in requirements analysis enables faster project initiation and more frequent requirement updates, supporting agile development methodologies at enterprise scale.
technicalPrerequisites: Natural Language Processing fundamentals including transformer architectures, attention mechanisms, and prompt engineering techniques
Ensemble learning methods, consensus algorithms, and multi-model aggregation strategies for reducing prediction variance
Software requirements engineering principles, IEEE-830 standards, and formal requirement specification methodologies
Document parsing and information extraction techniques including optical character recognition and structured data extraction
API design patterns, rate limiting, circuit breakers, and fault-tolerant distributed system architectures
Evaluation metrics for information extraction tasks including precision, recall, F1-score, and inter-annotator agreement
Hallucination detection methods in large language models and confidence estimation techniques for neural network outputs

keyTermsAndConcepts.definition: A structured requirements categorization methodology dividing requirements into Project (organizational context), Environment (external interfaces), Goals (stakeholder objectives), and System (functional/non-functional specifications). Provides semantic anchors that constrain LLM generation and improve extraction accuracy.

An ensemble approach aggregating outputs from multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq) using weighted voting and agreement scoring. Serves as runtime verification by leveraging model disagreement as a quality signal for hallucination detection.

A structured prompt engineering technique that decomposes requirement extraction into four focused sub-tasks corresponding to PEGS categories, each with explicit semantic boundaries and validation criteria embedded in prompt construction.

A quantitative measure of consensus between different LLM providers' outputs, computed using semantic similarity metrics and exact match ratios. Used as a confidence indicator and trigger for additional validation rounds.

A methodology for requirement extraction that maintains consistent performance across different software domains (academic, industrial, government) without domain-specific training or fine-tuning, achieved through structured prompting and template-based processing.

The capability to link extracted requirements to their source document locations and track forward connections to downstream development artifacts, enabling change impact analysis and audit trail maintenance.
Automated identification of LLM-generated content that is not supported by source documents, implemented through cross-model consistency checking and fact-verification against original text content.

Automated recovery procedures that activate when primary LLM providers fail or produce low-confidence outputs, including provider switching, prompt reformulation, and human review escalation based on configurable thresholds.

content.architecturalInsight: ReqFusion employs a pipeline architecture with distinct ingestion, processing, consensus, and output stages, each implementing the circuit breaker pattern for fault tolerance. The multi-provider orchestration layer abstracts provider-specific APIs behind a unified interface with request routing, load balancing, and failover capabilities. A persistent traceability layer maintains bidirectional links between source documents, extracted requirements, and downstream artifacts using graph-based data structures for efficient querying and impact analysis.
content.limitations: The approach is fundamentally limited by the quality and consistency of source documentation, with poor or ambiguous requirements specifications leading to degraded extraction accuracy. API dependency introduces latency variability and potential service interruptions, while consensus mechanisms may struggle with legitimate requirement variations rather than errors. The system requires manual ground truth annotation for domain-specific evaluation, and complex requirements spanning multiple PEGS categories may be incorrectly classified or fragmented during extraction.
content.comparisonToExisting: ReqFusion achieves 24% improvement in F1-score over generic prompting approaches (0.88 vs 0.71) and demonstrates 78% reduction in analysis time compared to manual methods. The multi-provider consensus mechanism provides higher reliability than single-model approaches while maintaining comparable latency through parallel processing. Unlike existing domain-specific tools, ReqFusion maintains consistent performance across academic, industrial, and government document types without requiring domain-specific training or customization.
relatedWork: IEEE-830 Software Requirements Specification standard and formal requirement engineering methodologies
Prompt engineering techniques for large language models including chain-of-thought and few-shot learning approaches
Ensemble methods for neural networks and multi-model consensus algorithms in machine learning
Information extraction from documents using transformer-based models and named entity recognition
Software traceability and change impact analysis tools for requirements management
Hallucination detection and mitigation strategies in large language model applications
content.implementationNotes: Implementation would leverage the OpenAI, Anthropic, and Groq Python SDKs for API integration, with document parsing using pypdf2, python-docx, and python-pptx libraries. FastAPI or Flask could provide the web service layer, while Celery with Redis enables asynchronous job processing for batch workflows. PostgreSQL with JSONB columns would efficiently store requirement hierarchies and traceability metadata. A reference implementation could start with a single-provider version using OpenAI's API, gradually adding consensus mechanisms and additional providers as the system matures.
